title: Assets vs Liabilities
concept: Assets vs liabilities, rent vs buy
summary: A simple lens for money decisions: assets tend to put money in your pocket over time, liabilities take it out. Many things we treat as 'investments' are actually lifestyle liabilities.

sections.heading: The simple test
sections.body: Ask: over time, does this put money into my pocket or take money out? Assets (like investments, or skills that raise your income) add; liabilities (like a car or phone on EMI) subtract through depreciation and running costs.

sections.heading: Things that masquerade as assets
sections.body: A car is usually a convenience, not an investment. A phone on EMI is a lifestyle liability. A property is an asset only if the numbers — rent, EMI, maintenance — actually work out.

sections.heading: Skills as an asset
sections.body: Spending on genuine upskilling can be one of the best 'assets' early in a career, because a higher income compounds across every future year.

quiz.prompt: In this simple lens, an asset is something that over time:
options: Takes money out of your pocket

quiz.prompt: A phone bought on EMI is best described as:
The simple test for telling an asset from a liability is to ask whether, over time, it:
options: Puts money into or takes money out of your pocket

quiz.prompt: According to the lesson, one of the best 'assets' early in a career is:
options: Spending on genuine upskilling that raises your income

quiz.prompt: A property counts as a genuine asset only when:
options: It is in a big city
The numbers — rent, EMI, maintenance — actually work out

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
